attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Heraclitus
attributes.bioShort: Ephesian philosopher known for the doctrine of universal flux — "everything flows" (panta rhei) — and the unity of opposites. His Logos doctrine posits a rational principle governing all change. Survives only in fragments cited by later authors.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Parmenides
attributes.bioShort: Eleatic philosopher who argued in verse that Being is one, unchanging, and indivisible — and that plurality and change are illusions arising from the senses. His challenge to Heraclitean flux set the terms for all subsequent Greek metaphysics.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Democritus
attributes.bioShort: Developed atomic theory alongside Leucippus: all of reality consists of indivisible atoms moving through void. Also wrote extensively on ethics, epistemology, and mathematics, though most works survive only in fragments.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Zeno of Citium
attributes.bioShort: Founder of Stoicism, who taught in the Stoa Poikile (Painted Porch) in Athens. Held that living according to nature and reason, and cultivating virtue as the sole good, is the path to happiness. His writings survive only in fragments.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Socrates
attributes.bioShort: Athenian philosopher who wrote nothing himself but shaped Western thought through his method of questioning (elenchus). Tried and executed for impiety and corrupting youth.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Plato
attributes.bioShort: Student of Socrates and teacher of Aristotle. Founded the Academy in Athens. Developed the theory of Forms and wrote in dialogue form, with Socrates as the central interlocutor.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Aristotle
attributes.bioShort: Student of Plato and tutor of Alexander the Great. Founded the Lyceum. His works range across logic, biology, physics, ethics, politics, and poetics.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Epicurus
attributes.bioShort: Founder of the Garden school in Athens. Taught that the good life consists in ataraxia and aponia, achieved through friendship, modest pleasures, and freedom from fear.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Marcus Aurelius
attributes.bioShort: Roman Emperor and Stoic philosopher. His private journal, the Meditations, was never intended for publication and remains one of the most direct expressions of Stoic practice.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Plotinus
attributes.bioShort: Founder of Neoplatonism. His Enneads (edited by Porphyry) articulate a hierarchical emanationist metaphysics: from the One proceeds Intellect (Nous), then Soul, then Matter. Deeply influential on Augustine and on Islamic and Jewish philosophy.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Augustine of Hippo
attributes.bioShort: Bishop of Hippo and the most influential theologian of Western Christianity. Synthesized Neoplatonism with Christian doctrine. His Confessions pioneered the philosophical autobiography; The City of God articulated the theology of history.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Thomas Aquinas
attributes.bioShort: Dominican friar and the defining philosopher of Scholasticism. His synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy with Christian theology — Thomism — remains the official philosophical framework of the Catholic Church.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: William of Ockham
attributes.bioShort: Franciscan philosopher and nominalist. "Ockham's Razor" (entities should not be multiplied beyond necessity) challenges realism about universals. His nominalism and anti-papal political writings made him a controversial figure.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: John Buridan
attributes.bioShort: Parisian arts master and the most influential medieval logician of the 14th century. His Tractatus de consequentiis and Summulae de dialectica systematised modal syllogistic, the theory of consequences, and supposition theory. Sceptical nominalist in Ockham's wake; the so-called "Buridan's ass" thought experiment is mis-attributed but illustrates his interest in indifferent choice.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Francis Bacon
attributes.bioShort: Statesman and philosopher who championed inductive method and empirical science over Aristotelian scholasticism. His Novum Organum argues for systematic observation and experiment as the basis of natural knowledge.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Thomas Hobbes
attributes.bioShort: Materialist philosopher and political theorist. Leviathan argues that without a sovereign authority, human life would be "solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short," grounding political obligation in a social contract.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Baruch Spinoza
attributes.bioShort: Rationalist metaphysician who argued that God and Nature are identical (Deus sive Natura) — one infinite substance with infinite attributes. Excommunicated from the Amsterdam Jewish community. His Ethics is written in geometric form.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: John Locke
attributes.bioShort: Father of classical liberalism and British empiricism. The Essay Concerning Human Understanding argues the mind is a tabula rasa; the Two Treatises ground government in consent and the protection of natural rights.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz
attributes.bioShort: Polymath who independently invented the calculus (alongside Newton) and developed a rationalist metaphysics of monads — simple, windowless substances — and pre-established harmony. His theodicy argues this is the best of all possible worlds.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: George Berkeley
attributes.bioShort: Idealist philosopher who argued that material substance is a fiction — to be is to be perceived (esse est percipi). Only minds and their ideas exist. His immaterialism was partly a defense of common sense against Lockean skepticism.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: René Descartes
attributes.bioShort: Mathematician and philosopher who sought to rebuild knowledge on indubitable foundations. The cogito argument and mind-body dualism are his most influential contributions.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
attributes.bioShort: Political philosopher and critic of civilization who argued that humans are naturally good but corrupted by society. The Social Contract ("Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains") and the Discourse on Inequality were formative for the French Revolution and Kant.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: David Hume
attributes.bioShort: Empiricist philosopher and historian who argued that all knowledge derives from experience. His skepticism about causation and the self profoundly influenced Kant.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Immanuel Kant
attributes.bioShort: Central figure of the Enlightenment. The three Critiques attempted a synthesis of rationalism and empiricism, and founded the project of transcendental idealism.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Jeremy Bentham
attributes.bioShort: Founder of utilitarianism and legal reformer. Held that the greatest happiness of the greatest number is the measure of right and wrong. His auto-icon (preserved remains) is displayed at University College London.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: John Stuart Mill
attributes.bioShort: The preeminent Victorian philosopher, who refined Benthamite utilitarianism to distinguish quality of pleasures, defended individual liberty against social tyranny, and championed women's suffrage. His Logic systematized inductive method.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Arthur Schopenhauer
attributes.bioShort: Post-Kantian philosopher who argued that behind the phenomenal world lies a blind, striving Will — and that the good life consists in aesthetic contemplation and ascetic denial of the will. Influenced Nietzsche, Wagner, and Freud.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Franz Brentano
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher and psychologist who revived the Scholastic concept of intentionality: all mental acts are directed at objects. His Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint directly inspired Husserl's phenomenology.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Wilhelm Dilthey
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher of history and hermeneutics who argued that the human sciences (Geisteswissenschaften) require a distinct method — Verstehen (understanding) — irreducible to natural-scientific explanation. Foundational for Gadamer and Ricoeur.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
attributes.bioShort: Post-Kantian idealist who grounded Kant's system in the self-positing activity of the I (Ich). His Wissenschaftslehre attempts to derive the structure of experience from a single first principle. Hegel saw his position as one-sided subjective idealism.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: F. W. J. Schelling
attributes.bioShort: German Idealist who developed a philosophy of nature and an identity-philosophy treating the Absolute as the indifference point of subject and object. An early collaborator with Hegel; their relationship became strained after Hegel's rise.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: G. W. F. Hegel
attributes.bioShort: Post-Kantian idealist who developed a dialectical account of history and spirit (Geist). His system — spanning logic, nature, and mind — argues that reality is the self-development of Absolute Spirit coming to know itself through human history, culture, and philosophy. His influence ramifies across Marxism, existentialism, analytic philosophy, critical theory, and hermeneutics.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Søren Kierkegaard
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher and theologian who wrote under multiple pseudonyms to enact rather than merely describe the existential stages of life. His critique of Hegel's System — that it leaves no room for the existing, choosing, suffering individual — is the founding gesture of existentialism.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Karl Marx
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher, economist, and revolutionary who inverted Hegel's idealist dialectic: the motor of history is not Spirit's self-recognition but the material forces and relations of production. Co-author (with Engels) of the Communist Manifesto; author of Capital.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Friedrich Nietzsche
attributes.bioShort: Critic of morality, religion, and systematic philosophy. Introduced concepts of the will to power, eternal recurrence, and the Übermensch. Wrote in aphorism and polemic.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Gottlob Frege
attributes.bioShort: Mathematician-logician who founded modern logic and the philosophy of language. His Begriffsschrift (1879) invented quantificational logic; the distinction between Sinn (sense) and Bedeutung (reference) is one of the most productive in analytic philosophy. Russell's paradox undermined his logicist program.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Charles Sanders Peirce
attributes.bioShort: Founder of pragmatism (which he later renamed pragmaticism to distinguish it from James's version). Contributed to logic, semiotics, and the philosophy of science. His maxim: the meaning of a concept lies in its conceivable practical effects.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: William James
attributes.bioShort: Psychologist and philosopher who popularized pragmatism and developed radical empiricism. His Principles of Psychology (1890) founded scientific psychology in America; Pragmatism (1907) made the movement a cultural force. Also wrote on religious experience and the will to believe.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: John Dewey
attributes.bioShort: The most influential American philosopher of the twentieth century. Developed instrumentalism — knowledge as a tool for solving problems — and applied it to education, democracy, and social reform. His Logic: The Theory of Inquiry is the fullest statement of his naturalism.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: George Herbert Mead
attributes.bioShort: Social philosopher and pragmatist who developed a theory of the self as constituted through social interaction and language. Mind, Self, and Society (compiled from student notes) is the foundational text of symbolic interactionism.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: C. I. Lewis
attributes.bioShort: Pragmatist philosopher who founded modern modal logic and developed conceptualistic pragmatism — the view that the categories we apply to experience are pragmatically chosen frameworks. A crucial bridge between the classical pragmatists and Quine's generation.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Bertrand Russell
attributes.bioShort: Co-founder of analytic philosophy and mathematical logic. With Whitehead, wrote Principia Mathematica attempting to ground arithmetic in logic. His theory of descriptions, logical atomism, and prolific popular writings on ethics, education, and politics shaped the twentieth century.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: G. E. Moore
attributes.bioShort: Co-founder of analytic philosophy whose Principia Ethica argued that "good" is a simple, non-natural property — defining it in natural terms commits the "naturalistic fallacy." His commonsensism and attention to ordinary language were also highly influential.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Alfred North Whitehead
attributes.bioShort: Mathematician and philosopher who co-authored Principia Mathematica with Russell before developing process philosophy — a metaphysics in which events and processes, not substances, are fundamental. Process and Reality remains his major metaphysical work.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Ludwig Wittgenstein
attributes.bioShort: Two distinct phases: the Tractatus (logical atomism, picture theory of meaning) and the Investigations (language games, family resemblance, anti-essentialism). Perhaps the most discussed philosopher of the twentieth century.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Moritz Schlick
attributes.bioShort: Founder of the Vienna Circle and the leading figure of logical positivism. Held that philosophy's task is the clarification of meaning via the verification principle. Assassinated on the steps of the University of Vienna by a former student.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Rudolf Carnap
attributes.bioShort: The most technically sophisticated member of the Vienna Circle. Developed the program of rational reconstruction, inductive logic, and linguistic frameworks. His distinction between internal and external questions anticipates later debates about ontology. Quine's "Two Dogmas" is principally directed against Carnap.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: A. J. Ayer
attributes.bioShort: Brought logical positivism to a British audience with Language, Truth and Logic (1936), written at age 24. The verification criterion of meaning — that a statement is meaningful only if it is analytic or empirically verifiable — became a touchstone for and target of subsequent philosophy.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Gilbert Ryle
attributes.bioShort: Oxford philosopher whose Concept of Mind (1949) attacked Descartes's "ghost in the machine" by arguing that mind-talk describes dispositions to behave, not a separate mental substance. Coined the term "category mistake."

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: J. L. Austin
attributes.bioShort: Oxford philosopher who developed speech act theory — the distinction between locutionary, illocutionary, and perlocutionary acts. How to Do Things with Words articulated what we do with language beyond describing the world. Died at 48, leaving much unwritten.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: P. F. Strawson
attributes.bioShort: Oxford philosopher who challenged Russell's theory of descriptions, developed a descriptive metaphysics of persons and material bodies, and wrote a landmark critique of Kant. His "Freedom and Resentment" (1962) transformed the philosophy of moral responsibility.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: H. P. Grice
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher of language who distinguished what is said from what is implicated (conversational implicature) and articulated the cooperative maxims governing communication. "Logic and Conversation" is among the most cited papers in philosophy of language.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Edmund Husserl
attributes.bioShort: Founder of phenomenology. Developed the phenomenological method — bracketing the natural attitude to describe the essential structures of consciousness and its intentional objects. Influenced Heidegger, Merleau-Ponty, Sartre, and Levinas.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Martin Heidegger
attributes.bioShort: Student of Husserl who transformed phenomenology into an analysis of Being and human existence (Dasein). Being and Time (1927) is the most influential continental philosophy work of the century. His Nazi involvement remains deeply controversial.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Jean-Paul Sartre
attributes.bioShort: Existentialist philosopher, novelist, and playwright. Existence precedes essence: humans have no fixed nature, only radical freedom and the anguish of responsibility. Being and Nothingness (1943) is the systematic statement; "Existentialism is a Humanism" popularized the movement.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Maurice Merleau-Ponty
attributes.bioShort: Phenomenologist who argued that perception is fundamentally embodied — the lived body (corps propre) is the primary locus of experience, not the detached Cartesian mind. Phenomenology of Perception (1945) remains the key text in philosophy of embodiment.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Emmanuel Levinas
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher of ethics and alterity who argued that the encounter with the Other's face issues an infinite, asymmetrical ethical demand prior to all ontology. Totality and Infinity (1961) and Otherwise Than Being (1974) are the major works.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Hannah Arendt
attributes.bioShort: Political philosopher and historian of ideas. The Origins of Totalitarianism, The Human Condition (distinguishing labor, work, and action), and Eichmann in Jerusalem (coining "the banality of evil") are her three most influential works.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Hans-Georg Gadamer
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher of hermeneutics whose Truth and Method (1960) argued that understanding is always historically situated, mediated by tradition and language, and irreducible to method. The "fusion of horizons" is his model of how past and present meet in interpretation.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Paul Ricoeur
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher who combined phenomenology, hermeneutics, and narrative theory. Time and Narrative argues that human experience is fundamentally temporal and story-shaped; Oneself as Another develops a hermeneutics of personal identity.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Michel Foucault
attributes.bioShort: Historian of ideas and theorist of power-knowledge. His genealogical analyses of madness, medicine, the prison, and sexuality showed how regimes of truth shape and constrain human subjects. Among the most cited scholars in the humanities.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Jacques Derrida
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher who developed deconstruction — a reading practice exposing the hidden binary oppositions and aporias in texts, including philosophical ones. Of Grammatology (1967) and Writing and Difference are foundational. His work generated enormous controversy in Anglophone academia.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Jürgen Habermas
attributes.bioShort: The leading second-generation Frankfurt School theorist. The Theory of Communicative Action grounds critical theory in an analysis of communicative rationality and the pragmatics of discourse. Has engaged extensively with analytic philosophy, Rawls, and Rorty.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Theodor W. Adorno
attributes.bioShort: Frankfurt School philosopher, musicologist, and cultural critic. Co-authored Dialectic of Enlightenment with Horkheimer; wrote Negative Dialectics as an extended polemic against identity thinking. His aesthetics remains one of the richest in the twentieth century.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Walter Benjamin
attributes.bioShort: Critic, essayist, and philosopher affiliated with the Frankfurt School. His essays on the aura of art, the flaneur, and messianic historical time are among the most read in twentieth-century cultural theory. Died fleeing the Nazis at the Spanish border.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Herbert Marcuse
attributes.bioShort: Frankfurt School philosopher whose Eros and Civilization synthesized Freud and Marx, and One-Dimensional Man diagnosed late-capitalist society's repression of critical thought. A major intellectual influence on the New Left and 1968 movements.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Gilles Deleuze
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher who developed a philosophy of difference, multiplicity, and becoming against representational and identity-based thinking. Difference and Repetition (1968) and the two volumes of Capitalism and Schizophrenia (with Guattari) are his major works.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Simone de Beauvoir
attributes.bioShort: Existentialist philosopher and feminist theorist. The Second Sex established the framework of woman as Other and laid groundwork for second-wave feminism.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: W. V. O. Quine
attributes.bioShort: The most influential American analytic philosopher of the mid-century. "Two Dogmas of Empiricism" demolished the analytic-synthetic distinction and verification theory; Word and Object argued for ontological relativity. His naturalism made epistemology continuous with science.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Nelson Goodman
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher of logic, language, and art. Fact, Fiction, and Forecast posed the "new riddle of induction" (grue); Languages of Art developed a symbol-theoretic account of the arts; Ways of Worldmaking advanced a radical irrealism about multiple world-versions.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Ruth Barcan Marcus
attributes.bioShort: Logician and philosopher who pioneered quantified modal logic and argued for the necessity of identity and direct reference — anticipating by decades many positions Kripke would later make famous. The "Barcan formula" is named for her.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Donald Davidson
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher of action, mind, and language. "Truth and Meaning" proposed truth-theoretic semantics; "Mental Events" defended anomalous monism; "On the Very Idea of a Conceptual Scheme" argued against the scheme-content dualism that Quine had inherited.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Saul Kripke
attributes.bioShort: Logician and philosopher who revolutionized modal logic (Kripke semantics) and the philosophy of language. Naming and Necessity (1972/1980) established rigid designators, a posteriori necessity, and the causal theory of reference. Wittgenstein on Rules challenged the rule-following sections of the Investigations.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Hilary Putnam
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher who passed through functionalism, scientific realism, internal realism, and pragmatism, always changing his mind in public. "The Meaning of Meaning" introduced Twin Earth and semantic externalism; his later pragmatism engaged seriously with Wittgenstein, Dewey, and James.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Michael Dummett
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher of language and logic who argued that anti-realism about a domain follows from an anti-realist semantics based on proof conditions rather than truth conditions. His massive Frege scholarship and his debate with Realism dominated Anglo-American philosophy of language for decades.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: David Lewis
attributes.bioShort: Metaphysician who defended modal realism — the thesis that all possible worlds are equally real, concrete, and spatiotemporally isolated from one another. On the Plurality of Worlds is the systematic statement; his shorter papers on causation, mind, and semantics are among the most technically accomplished in analytic philosophy.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: D. M. Armstrong
attributes.bioShort: Australian metaphysician and leading defender of a naturalist ontology of universals and states of affairs. Universals and Scientific Realism and A World of States of Affairs are the major works. His correspondence theory of truth and views on laws of nature were highly influential.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: G. E. M. Anscombe
attributes.bioShort: Wittgenstein's student and literary executor. Intention (1957) founded the philosophy of action; "Modern Moral Philosophy" (1958) coined the term "consequentialism," criticized Kantian ethics, and called for a return to Aristotelian virtue ethics. A committed Catholic philosopher.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Philippa Foot
attributes.bioShort: Moral philosopher who revived Aristotelian virtue ethics against Humean expressivism and Kantian formalism. Natural Goodness (2001) grounds ethics in a naturalistic account of what is good for living things of a given kind. Invented the trolley problem in a 1967 paper.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Bernard Williams
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher who subjected moral theory — especially utilitarianism and Kantian ethics — to sustained critique. Ethics and the Limits of Philosophy argues that moral philosophy cannot provide a systematic theory of how to live; Shame and Necessity recovers Greek moral psychology.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: John Rawls
attributes.bioShort: The most important political philosopher of the twentieth century. A Theory of Justice (1971) revived social contract theory against utilitarianism, using the "veil of ignorance" to derive principles of justice as fairness. Political Liberalism later reframed this as a political rather than a comprehensive doctrine.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Robert Nozick
attributes.bioShort: Libertarian political philosopher whose Anarchy, State, and Utopia (1974) argued that any state more extensive than a minimal nightwatchman state violates individual rights. Written as an explicit response to Rawls, it remains the most sophisticated defense of libertarianism.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Alasdair MacIntyre
attributes.bioShort: Moral philosopher whose After Virtue (1981) diagnosed the fragmentation of modern moral discourse and proposed a return to Aristotelian virtue ethics embedded in living traditions. A convert to Thomism, he developed this position through Whose Justice? Which Rationality? and Three Rival Versions.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Martha Nussbaum
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher who developed the capabilities approach to justice and human development alongside Amartya Sen. The Fragility of Goodness recovers Aristotle on luck and ethics; Upheavals of Thought is a major study of the cognitive role of emotions. Writes on classical literature, cosmopolitanism, and the law.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Peter Singer
attributes.bioShort: Utilitarian moral philosopher whose Animal Liberation (1975) founded the modern animal rights movement by arguing that species membership is morally arbitrary. Practical Ethics applies utilitarian reasoning to global poverty, infanticide, and euthanasia. One of the most publicly influential living philosophers.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Charles Taylor
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher whose Sources of the Self traced the modern identity's constitutive moral frameworks; The Ethics of Authenticity critiques the culture of self-fulfillment; A Secular Age is a monumental account of how the West moved from enchanted to secular conditions. A communitarian critic of Rawlsian liberalism.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: John Searle
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher of mind and language. Developed speech act theory beyond Austin; the Chinese Room argument challenged computational functionalism; Intentionality and The Construction of Social Reality address the mind's directedness and the ontology of social institutions.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Karl Popper
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher of science who replaced the verificationist criterion of meaning with falsifiability: a theory is scientific if it can in principle be refuted by observation. The Logic of Scientific Discovery and Conjectures and Refutations are the key texts. The Open Society and Its Enemies attacks Plato, Hegel, and Marx as totalitarian thinkers.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Thomas Kuhn
attributes.bioShort: Historian and philosopher of science whose Structure of Scientific Revolutions (1962) introduced "paradigm," "normal science," and "scientific revolution" into the culture. His arguments for incommensurability between paradigms triggered decades of debate about rationality and relativism.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Wilfrid Sellars
attributes.bioShort: Analytic philosopher whose attack on the Myth of the Given and vision of the "space of reasons" laid the groundwork for the Pittsburgh neo-Hegelian program. "Empiricism and the Philosophy of Mind" (1956) is the pivotal text.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Richard Rorty
attributes.bioShort: Philosophy and the Mirror of Nature (1979) attacked the foundations of analytic epistemology and proposed a neo-pragmatist alternative. Later developed a liberal irony combining solidarity with the contingency of all vocabularies. Aimed to heal the rift between the analytic and continental traditions.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Robert Brandom
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher of language and inferentialist. Making It Explicit (1994) develops Sellars's and Hegel's normative pragmatics into a systematic theory of conceptual content grounded in social practices of giving and asking for reasons.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: John McDowell
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher who draws on Hegel, Aristotle, Wittgenstein, and Evans to argue that conceptual content is unbounded — perception itself is already conceptually shaped. Mind and World (1994) is the central text.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Derek Parfit
attributes.bioShort: Moral philosopher who argued that personal identity over time is not what matters and that population ethics is a serious problem for any moral theory. Reasons and Persons (1984) and the posthumous On What Matters are among the most ambitious works in recent moral philosophy.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Thomas Nagel
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher known for "What Is It Like to Be a Bat?" (1974) — arguing that subjective experience cannot be captured by objective physical description — and The View from Nowhere, which explores the tension between the personal and impersonal standpoints.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Daniel Dennett
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher of mind and cognitive science who defended heterophenomenology and a deflationary account of consciousness. Consciousness Explained (1991) and Darwin's Dangerous Idea argued for a thoroughly naturalist picture of mind and meaning.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: David Chalmers
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher of mind who coined the "hard problem of consciousness" — why any physical process should give rise to subjective experience at all. The Conscious Mind (1996) defends a form of property dualism; his recent work turns to virtual reality and extended mind.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Tyler Burge
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher of mind and language who developed anti-individualism (social externalism): the contents of mental states are not determined by what is in the head but by the social and physical environment. "Individualism and the Mental" is the locus classicus.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Timothy Williamson
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher known for Knowledge and Its Limits (2000), which argues that knowledge — not justified true belief — is the fundamental epistemic concept, and for a sustained defense of modal logic and metaphysics against anti-metaphysical tendencies in analytic philosophy.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Frank Jackson
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher of mind whose "Mary's Room" thought experiment — Mary knows all physical facts about color perception but learns something new on seeing red for the first time — poses the knowledge argument against physicalism. Later recanted toward physicalism in From Metaphysics to Ethics.

attributes.label: Philosopher
attributes.name: Kit Fine
attributes.bioShort: Philosopher of logic and metaphysics who rehabilitated the notion of essence as real definition — irreducible to modal properties — in "Essence and Modality" (1994). His work on ontological dependence, vagueness, and truthmakers has been widely influential.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Presocratic Philosophy
attributes.description: The earliest Greek philosophers, seeking rational explanations of nature without appeal to mythology. Includes the Milesians, Heraclitus, Parmenides, and the Atomists.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Platonism
attributes.description: Philosophical tradition stemming from Plato, centered on the theory of Forms, the immortality of the soul, and the nature of knowledge as recollection.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Aristotelianism
attributes.description: Tradition derived from Aristotle, emphasizing empirical observation, logic, and the systematic study of nature, ethics, and politics.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Stoicism
attributes.description: School founded by Zeno of Citium, teaching that virtue is the only true good and that one should live in accordance with nature and reason.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Epicureanism
attributes.description: School founded by Epicurus, holding that the highest good is ataraxia (tranquility) and aponia (absence of pain), achievable through simple pleasures and philosophical friendship.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Neoplatonism
attributes.description: Late antique tradition founded by Plotinus, synthesizing Platonic metaphysics with Aristotelian and Stoic elements into a hierarchical emanationist system. Deeply influential on Christian, Jewish, and Islamic philosophy.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Scholasticism
attributes.description: Medieval philosophical and theological tradition reconciling Christian faith with classical (especially Aristotelian) reason. Associated with the cathedral schools and universities of Europe.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Rationalism
attributes.description: Early modern tradition holding that reason, rather than sensory experience, is the primary source of knowledge. Associated with Descartes, Spinoza, and Leibniz.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Empiricism
attributes.description: Tradition holding that knowledge derives primarily from sensory experience. Associated with Locke, Berkeley, and Hume.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Utilitarianism
attributes.description: Ethical theory holding that the right action maximizes overall well-being or happiness. Founded by Bentham, refined by Mill, and extended by Singer.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: German Idealism
attributes.description: Post-Kantian movement arguing that reality is fundamentally mental or conceptually structured. Includes Fichte, Schelling, and Hegel.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Existentialism
attributes.description: Movement centered on individual existence, freedom, and choice. Associated with Kierkegaard, Nietzsche, Sartre, and de Beauvoir.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Pragmatism
attributes.description: American philosophical tradition holding that the meaning of ideas lies in their practical consequences and that inquiry is a tool for resolving genuine doubt. Founded by Peirce; developed by James, Dewey, and Mead.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Phenomenology
attributes.description: Philosophical method focused on the first-person structure of experience. Founded by Husserl; extended and transformed by Heidegger, Merleau-Ponty, and Sartre.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Analytic Philosophy
attributes.description: Tradition emphasizing clarity, logical analysis, and attention to language. Dominant in Anglophone philosophy since the early twentieth century.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Logical Positivism
attributes.description: Vienna Circle movement holding that meaningful statements are either analytic (true by definition) or empirically verifiable. Associated with Schlick, Carnap, and Ayer. Largely dissolved after Quine's "Two Dogmas."

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Ordinary Language Philosophy
attributes.description: Oxford-centred movement holding that philosophical problems arise from misuse of ordinary language and dissolve under careful attention to how words are actually used. Associated with Ryle, Austin, Strawson, and Grice.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Hermeneutics
attributes.description: Theory and practice of interpretation, especially of texts and historical expressions. Developed by Schleiermacher, Dilthey, Heidegger, and Gadamer into a general philosophical account of human understanding.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Critical Theory
attributes.description: Tradition associated with the Frankfurt School, synthesizing Hegel, Marx, Freud, and Weber to critique ideology and the conditions for human emancipation. Associated with Horkheimer, Adorno, and Habermas.

attributes.label: School
attributes.name: Neo-Pragmatism
attributes.description: Late-twentieth-century revival and transformation of classical pragmatism, combining it with analytic philosophy of language and Hegelian themes. Associated with Rorty, Brandom, and McDowell.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: On Nature
attributes.descriptionShort: Surviving in fragments, this philosophical poem in two parts presents the Way of Truth (Being is one, unchanging, indivisible) and the Way of Seeming (a cosmology offered as merely plausible).

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Fragments
attributes.descriptionShort: Around 125 fragments survive, cited by later authors. They articulate the doctrine of universal flux, the unity of opposites, and the Logos as the rational principle governing all change.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Republic
attributes.descriptionShort: Plato's central work on justice, the ideal city-state, the philosopher-king, and the theory of Forms. Contains the allegory of the cave.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Phaedo
attributes.descriptionShort: The dialogue set on the day of Socrates's death. Argues for the immortality of the soul through the Theory of Recollection and the Theory of Forms.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Symposium
attributes.descriptionShort: A series of speeches on the nature of Eros (love), culminating in Socrates's account of the Ladder of Beauty and Diotima's teaching on the Form of Beauty.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Nicomachean Ethics
attributes.descriptionShort: Aristotle's systematic account of the good life, virtue, practical wisdom (phronesis), and eudaimonia (flourishing).

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Metaphysics
attributes.descriptionShort: Aristotle's investigation of Being qua Being — substance, form, matter, actuality, and potentiality. Book XII on the Unmoved Mover is the theological culmination.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Organon
attributes.descriptionShort: The collection of Aristotle's logical works: Categories, On Interpretation, Prior Analytics, Posterior Analytics, Topics, and Sophistical Refutations. Dominated logic until Frege.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Meditations
attributes.descriptionShort: A private journal never intended for publication. Reflections on Stoic practice, duty, impermanence, and the philosopher's inner discipline.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Enneads
attributes.descriptionShort: Fifty-four treatises organized by Porphyry into six groups of nine. Develops the Neoplatonic system: the One, Intellect (Nous), Soul, and their emanation and return.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Confessions
attributes.descriptionShort: Philosophical autobiography and prayer addressed to God. The first of its kind in Western literature. Narrates Augustine's intellectual journey through Manichaeism and Neoplatonism to Christianity, and contains deep meditations on time, memory, and the will.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: City of God
attributes.descriptionShort: Written after the sack of Rome (410), argues that the earthly city and the city of God are intermingled in history but distinct in destiny. Shaped Western Christian political theology.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Summa Theologiae
attributes.descriptionShort: The masterwork of Scholastic theology. Organized as objections and replies, it covers God's existence and nature, creation, virtue, law, and the sacraments. Contains the Five Ways (proofs for God's existence).

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Summa Logicae
attributes.descriptionShort: A comprehensive treatment of logic from a nominalist standpoint. Argues that universals are mental concepts (not real entities) and that terms have meaning through their function in propositions, not through metaphysical correspondence.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Novum Organum
attributes.descriptionShort: Proposes a new inductive method for natural philosophy, replacing Aristotelian syllogistic. Introduces the "idols" (of the tribe, cave, marketplace, and theater) as sources of intellectual error to be cleared away.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Leviathan
attributes.descriptionShort: Hobbes's masterpiece of political philosophy. In the state of nature life is "solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short"; rational agents contract to surrender natural freedom to a sovereign in exchange for peace and security.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Ethics
attributes.descriptionShort: Written in geometric form (definitions, axioms, propositions, proofs). Argues that God and Nature are one infinite substance (Deus sive Natura); minds and bodies are two attributes of this one substance; human freedom consists in understanding necessity.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: An Essay Concerning Human Understanding
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues that the mind at birth is a blank slate (tabula rasa) and that all knowledge derives from experience. Develops the distinction between primary and secondary qualities and a theory of personal identity.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Two Treatises of Government
attributes.descriptionShort: The Second Treatise grounds legitimate government in consent and the protection of natural rights to life, liberty, and property. Directly influenced the American founders.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Monadology
attributes.descriptionShort: Ninety paragraphs presenting Leibniz's mature metaphysics: reality consists of monads (simple, windowless, mind-like substances), their pre-established harmony, and God as the best of all possible worlds.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: A Treatise Concerning the Principles of Human Knowledge
attributes.descriptionShort: The systematic statement of Berkeley's immaterialism: material substance is unintelligible; to be is to be perceived (esse est percipi); God perceives all things at all times.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Meditations on First Philosophy
attributes.descriptionShort: Six meditations establishing the cogito, arguing for mind-body dualism, and attempting to ground knowledge on indubitable foundations.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Social Contract
attributes.descriptionShort: "Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains." Argues that legitimate political authority rests on a social contract expressing the general will, not on tradition or divine right.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Discourse on the Origin of Inequality
attributes.descriptionShort: Distinguishes natural inequality (physical differences) from moral inequality (property, law, power) and argues the latter is a contingent product of civilization, not of nature.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding
attributes.descriptionShort: Hume's accessible revision of the Treatise. Presents the copy principle, the problem of induction, skepticism about causation, and the argument against miracles.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: A Treatise of Human Nature
attributes.descriptionShort: Hume's first and most systematic work, "fallen dead-born from the press." Books I (Understanding) and II (Passions) develop his associationist psychology; Book III (Morals) argues that moral judgments express sentiments, not reason.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Critique of Pure Reason
attributes.descriptionShort: Kant's first Critique. Argues that space, time, and the categories of understanding are imposed by the mind on experience — the Copernican Revolution in philosophy.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
attributes.descriptionShort: Kant's foundational work in moral philosophy. Articulates the categorical imperative and grounds morality in pure practical reason rather than consequences or inclination.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Critique of the Power of Judgment
attributes.descriptionShort: Kant's third Critique, on aesthetic and teleological judgment. The Analytic of the Beautiful grounds aesthetic experience in a free play of imagination and understanding; the section on the sublime is also widely discussed.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation
attributes.descriptionShort: The founding text of utilitarianism: "Nature has placed mankind under the governance of two sovereign masters, pain and pleasure." Develops the felicific calculus for measuring utility.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Utilitarianism
attributes.descriptionShort: Refines Bentham's utilitarianism by distinguishing qualities of pleasure ("better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied") and grounding the greatest happiness principle in a proof from the psychology of desire.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: On Liberty
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues for the harm principle: the only legitimate reason to restrict individual liberty is to prevent harm to others. A foundational text of political liberalism.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The World as Will and Representation
attributes.originalTitle: Die Welt als Wille und Vorstellung
attributes.descriptionShort: Schopenhauer's magnum opus: the phenomenal world is representation; the thing-in-itself is Will — a blind, striving force. Salvation lies in aesthetic contemplation and ascetic denial of the will-to-live. Directly influenced Nietzsche, Wagner, and Freud.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint
attributes.descriptionShort: Revives the Scholastic concept of intentionality as the mark of the mental: every mental act is directed at an object. The starting point for Husserl's phenomenology and much subsequent philosophy of mind.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: How to Make Our Ideas Clear
attributes.descriptionShort: The classic statement of the pragmatic maxim: the meaning of a concept lies in its conceivable practical effects. "Consider what effects, that might conceivably have practical bearings, we conceive the object of our conception to have."

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Pragmatism: A New Name for Some Old Ways of Thinking
attributes.descriptionShort: Eight lectures that brought pragmatism to a general audience. Argues that ideas are true insofar as they help us get into satisfactory relations with other parts of our experience — a thesis that drew fierce criticism from Russell and Moore.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Principles of Psychology
attributes.descriptionShort: The founding text of scientific psychology in America. Introduces the stream of consciousness, habit, emotion (the James–Lange theory), and the self. Written with extraordinary literary verve.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Experience and Nature
attributes.descriptionShort: Dewey's metaphysics of experience: experience is not a veil separating us from nature but is continuous with it. The work argues for a naturalist account of mind, language, and value.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Logic: The Theory of Inquiry
attributes.descriptionShort: The fullest statement of Dewey's instrumentalism about logic: inquiry is a controlled transformation of an indeterminate situation into a determinate one; logic is the theory of the methods that govern this process.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Mind, Self, and Society
attributes.descriptionShort: Compiled from student notes after Mead's death. Argues that mind and self emerge from social interaction and the use of significant symbols; the "I" and the "me" are both aspects of a self constituted through taking the attitude of the other.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Mind and the World Order
attributes.descriptionShort: Develops conceptualistic pragmatism: the a priori consists of pragmatically adopted conceptual frameworks applied to the given in experience. The given is real but valueless without interpretation; interpretation is always revisable.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Begriffsschrift
attributes.descriptionShort: Invented modern quantificational logic, introducing the universal quantifier, function-argument notation, and a complete formal system for logic. The single most important advance in logic since Aristotle.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Foundations of Arithmetic
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues that numbers are logical objects — extensions of concepts — and that arithmetic is reducible to logic. Contains the context principle and the critique of psychologism.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: On Sense and Reference
attributes.descriptionShort: Distinguishes the sense (Sinn) of an expression — its mode of presentation — from its reference (Bedeutung) — the object it picks out. "Hesperus is Phosphorus" is informative because the names have different senses but the same reference (Venus).

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Problems of Philosophy
attributes.descriptionShort: A clear and accessible introduction to Russell's realist empiricism. Argues for a distinction between knowledge by acquaintance and knowledge by description, and defends the existence of universals.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: On Denoting
attributes.descriptionShort: Russell's theory of descriptions: "The present King of France is bald" is not about a nonexistent entity but is a false existential claim. Called by Russell "a paradigm of philosophy" — solving apparent puzzles about reference through logical analysis.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Principia Ethica
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues that "good" is a simple, indefinable, non-natural property and that defining it in terms of anything natural commits the "naturalistic fallacy." Proposes an intuitionist ethics and identifies beauty and friendship as the highest goods.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Process and Reality
attributes.descriptionShort: Whitehead's mature metaphysics of process: the basic units of reality are "actual occasions" (events), not substances. Developed into the tradition of process theology.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Foundations of the Science of Knowledge
attributes.descriptionShort: Fichte's attempt to ground Kant's critical philosophy in the self-positing activity of the I (Ich). The foundation of his Wissenschaftslehre and the immediate predecessor to Schelling and Hegel.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: System of Transcendental Idealism
attributes.descriptionShort: Traces the development of consciousness from nature to self-consciousness and presents art as the objective expression of the Absolute. Schelling's most widely read work.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Phenomenology of Spirit
attributes.descriptionShort: Hegel's account of consciousness's journey toward Absolute Knowledge. Contains the master-slave dialectic, the unhappy consciousness, and the logic of Spirit unfolding through history and culture.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Science of Logic
attributes.descriptionShort: Hegel's systematic treatment of the pure categories of thought — Being, Essence, and Concept — as the self-movement of the Absolute Idea. The most demanding of his major works.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Encyclopedia Logic
attributes.originalTitle: Enzyklopädie der philosophischen Wissenschaften, Teil I
attributes.descriptionShort: Part I of the Encyclopedia of Philosophical Sciences — the "Shorter Logic." A compressed presentation of the logical system. The most accessible entry into Hegel's logic.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Elements of the Philosophy of Right
attributes.descriptionShort: Hegel's social and political philosophy, covering abstract right, morality, and ethical life (Sittlichkeit) — family, civil society, and the state. The most politically contested of his works.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Lectures on the History of Philosophy
attributes.originalTitle: Vorlesungen über die Geschichte der Philosophie
attributes.descriptionShort: Posthumously compiled lectures tracing philosophy from the pre-Socratics to Kant and Hegel himself, as the progressive self-realization of Spirit.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Either/Or
attributes.descriptionShort: Published pseudonymously as Victor Eremita. Contrasts the aesthetic and ethical stages of existence through the papers of two fictional authors.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Concluding Unscientific Postscript
attributes.descriptionShort: Published as Johannes Climacus. The major statement of Kierkegaard's existential philosophy and sustained critique of Hegel. Introduces "subjectivity is truth."

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Economic and Philosophic Manuscripts of 1844
attributes.descriptionShort: Unpublished notebooks developing Marx's early concepts of alienated labor, species-being, and the critical relationship between his materialism and Hegel's idealism.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Capital, Volume I
attributes.descriptionShort: Marx's critique of political economy. Analyzes the commodity, labor-value, surplus-value, and capital accumulation — with an explicit methodological debt to Hegel's dialectical logic.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Beyond Good and Evil
attributes.descriptionShort: Nietzsche's critique of past philosophers as unconscious advocates for their own prejudices. Develops the will to power and the notion of the philosopher of the future.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: On the Genealogy of Morality
attributes.descriptionShort: Three essays tracing the historical origins of our moral concepts: the master-slave inversion, bad conscience, and the ascetic ideal. The model for Foucault's genealogical method.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus
attributes.descriptionShort: Wittgenstein's early masterpiece. A numbered series of propositions arguing that language pictures facts and that what cannot be said must be passed over in silence.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Philosophical Investigations
attributes.descriptionShort: Published posthumously. Dismantles the picture theory of meaning via language games, family resemblance, and the private language argument.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: General Theory of Knowledge
attributes.descriptionShort: Schlick's early epistemological work, combining a correspondence theory of truth with an analysis of the conditions of scientific knowledge. Later superseded by his verificationism.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Logical Structure of the World
attributes.descriptionShort: Attempts a rational reconstruction of all knowledge from elementary experiences using logical construction. The most ambitious statement of the positivist program.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Logical Syntax of Language
attributes.descriptionShort: Develops the principle of tolerance: there are no morals in logic, only conventions. Philosophy's task is the logical analysis of the language of science.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Language, Truth and Logic
attributes.descriptionShort: Written at 24, brought logical positivism to a British audience. The verification principle: a statement is cognitively meaningful only if it is analytic or empirically verifiable. Ethical statements express emotions, not truths.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Concept of Mind
attributes.descriptionShort: Attacks Cartesian dualism (the "ghost in the machine") as a category mistake. Mind-talk describes behavioral dispositions, not inner occurrences in a separate mental substance.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: How to Do Things with Words
attributes.descriptionShort: Based on the 1955 William James Lectures. Distinguishes locutionary, illocutionary, and perlocutionary acts, and argues that utterances are performances as much as descriptions.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Individuals: An Essay in Descriptive Metaphysics
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues that material bodies and persons are the basic particulars in our conceptual scheme. Introduces the distinction between revisionary and descriptive metaphysics.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Freedom and Resentment
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues that the concept of moral responsibility is tied to the "reactive attitudes" (resentment, gratitude, indignation) that are constitutive of interpersonal relationships, not to metaphysical facts about determinism.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Logic and Conversation
attributes.descriptionShort: Distinguishes what is said (semantic content) from what is implicated (conversational implicature) and articulates four maxims of cooperative conversation: Quantity, Quality, Relation, Manner.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Logical Investigations
attributes.descriptionShort: Husserl's anti-psychologistic critique of logic and the founding work of phenomenology. The Sixth Investigation on meaning-intention and meaning-fulfillment is especially influential.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Ideas I
attributes.descriptionShort: Introduces the phenomenological reduction (epoché) and the distinction between the natural attitude and pure consciousness. The most systematic statement of transcendental phenomenology.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Being and Time
attributes.descriptionShort: The fundamental ontology of Dasein (human existence). Analyzes being-in-the-world, care, temporality, authenticity, and thrownness. The most influential continental philosophy work of the twentieth century.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Question Concerning Technology
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues that the essence of modern technology is Gestell (enframing) — a mode of revealing that reduces everything to standing-reserve (Bestand). Art offers an alternative mode of revealing.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Being and Nothingness
attributes.descriptionShort: The systematic statement of Sartre's existentialism. Analyzes consciousness (being-for-itself), things (being-in-itself), and the look of the Other. Argues for radical freedom and the consequent anguish of responsibility.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Phenomenology of Perception
attributes.descriptionShort: The foundational text in philosophy of embodiment. Argues that perception is not a mental act but an achievement of the whole body; the lived body (corps propre) is the primary subject of experience.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Totality and Infinity
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues that ethics — the encounter with the Other's face — is first philosophy, prior to ontology. The face issues an infinite, asymmetrical demand: "Thou shalt not kill."

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Human Condition
attributes.descriptionShort: Distinguishes three fundamental human activities: labor (biological necessity), work (fabrication of durable objects), and action (political activity in the public realm). Diagnoses modernity's eclipse of genuine political action.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
attributes.descriptionShort: Traces the historical roots of Nazi and Stalinist totalitarianism through anti-Semitism and imperialism. Argues that totalitarianism is a genuinely novel form of domination, not merely tyranny.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Truth and Method
attributes.descriptionShort: The foundational text of philosophical hermeneutics. Argues that understanding is always historically situated, mediated by tradition and language, irreducible to methodological control. The fusion of horizons is the model of interpretation.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Oneself as Another
attributes.descriptionShort: A hermeneutics of personal identity that distinguishes idem-identity (sameness) from ipse-identity (selfhood). Argues that narrative and ethical commitment are constitutive of who a person is.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Discipline and Punish
attributes.descriptionShort: A genealogy of the modern prison and disciplinary power. Introduces the Panopticon as a model of a new form of power that operates through surveillance and normalization rather than spectacular punishment.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Order of Things
attributes.descriptionShort: An archaeological analysis of the epistemes (historical structures of knowledge) governing the human sciences. Ends with the famous prediction that "man" — a recent invention — may soon be erased "like a face drawn in sand at the edge of the sea."

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Of Grammatology
attributes.descriptionShort: Derrida's critique of "logocentrism" — the metaphysics of presence in Western philosophy from Plato to Saussure. Introduces différance and argues for the primacy of writing over speech.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Theory of Communicative Action
attributes.descriptionShort: A two-volume work grounding critical theory in an analysis of communicative rationality and the pragmatics of speech. Distinguishes communicative action (oriented to understanding) from strategic action (oriented to success).

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Dialectic of Enlightenment
attributes.descriptionShort: Co-authored with Max Horkheimer. Argues that the Enlightenment's project of rational domination over nature turns into domination over human beings, culminating in fascism and the culture industry.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Negative Dialectics
attributes.descriptionShort: A sustained polemic against identity thinking — the philosophical gesture that forces the non-identical into conceptual identity. Proposes a negative dialectics that preserves the non-conceptual remainder.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Illuminations
attributes.descriptionShort: Posthumous collection including "The Work of Art in the Age of Mechanical Reproduction," "The Storyteller," and "Theses on the Philosophy of History." The essay on aura is the most discussed.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: One-Dimensional Man
attributes.descriptionShort: Diagnoses advanced industrial society's repression of genuine critical thought through the creation of false needs and the integration of opposition into the system. A major text of the New Left.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Difference and Repetition
attributes.descriptionShort: Deleuze's doctoral thesis and major philosophical work. Argues that difference is primary and identity derivative; repetition is not the same event recurring but a genuine production of novelty.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Second Sex
attributes.descriptionShort: Foundational work in feminist philosophy. Argues that woman is constructed as the Other relative to man, and examines how this shapes women's lived experience.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Two Dogmas of Empiricism
attributes.descriptionShort: The most influential paper in postwar analytic philosophy. Demolishes the analytic-synthetic distinction and the reductionist verification theory, arguing for holism about meaning and the continuity of philosophy and science.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Word and Object
attributes.descriptionShort: Develops the indeterminacy of translation thesis through the "gavagai" thought experiment, argues for ontological relativity, and proposes a naturalized epistemology.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Fact, Fiction, and Forecast
attributes.descriptionShort: Poses the "new riddle of induction" through the predicate "grue" (green up to a time, then blue), showing that merely enumerative accounts of induction fail and that entrenchment in practice distinguishes projectible predicates.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Ways of Worldmaking
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues for irrealism: there is no ready-made world; rather, there are multiple right world-versions constructed through different symbol systems. Art, science, and perception are equally valid modes of world-making.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Essays on Actions and Events
attributes.descriptionShort: Collected papers including "Actions, Reasons, and Causes" and "Mental Events" (anomalous monism). Argues that mental events are identical with physical events but mental descriptions are not reducible to physical ones.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Inquiries into Truth and Interpretation
attributes.descriptionShort: Collected papers including "Truth and Meaning," "Radical Interpretation," and "On the Very Idea of a Conceptual Scheme." Develops truth-conditional semantics and the principle of charity.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Naming and Necessity
attributes.descriptionShort: Based on 1970 Princeton lectures. Argues for rigid designators, a posteriori necessity ("Water is H₂O"), and the causal theory of reference. Among the most influential philosophy books of the twentieth century.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Wittgenstein on Rules and Private Language
attributes.descriptionShort: An interpretation of Wittgenstein's rule-following considerations as a skeptical paradox about meaning, and a "skeptical solution" in terms of communal assertability conditions.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Reason, Truth and History
attributes.descriptionShort: Introduces the "brain in a vat" thought experiment as a refutation of external realism and develops internal realism: truth is idealized rational acceptability from a human perspective.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Frege: Philosophy of Language
attributes.descriptionShort: The most comprehensive treatment of Frege's philosophy of language in any language. Develops Dummett's own anti-realist semantics based on proof conditions alongside the scholarly reconstruction.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: On the Plurality of Worlds
attributes.descriptionShort: The systematic defense of modal realism: all possible worlds are equally real, concrete, and spatiotemporally isolated. Modality is explained by quantification over worlds; universals are replaced by set-theoretic constructs from possible individuals.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Counterfactuals
attributes.descriptionShort: Develops a possible-worlds semantics for counterfactual conditionals based on comparative similarity of worlds. The analysis is extended to causation in subsequent papers.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Universals and Scientific Realism
attributes.descriptionShort: A two-volume argument that universals (properties and relations) exist as immanent in their instances and that they are needed to ground laws of nature and the reliability of induction.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Empiricism and the Philosophy of Mind
attributes.descriptionShort: Sellars's pivotal essay demolishing the Myth of the Given — the idea that there can be epistemic episodes that are both causally efficacious and logically prior to all conceptual activity.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Philosophy and the Mirror of Nature
attributes.descriptionShort: Attacks the Cartesian-Kantian project of epistemology-as-first-philosophy and argues for a hermeneutic, edifying conception of philosophy without foundations. A turning point in late-twentieth-century philosophy.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Contingency, Irony, and Solidarity
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues that truth, selfhood, and community are all contingent; the liberal ironist holds her final vocabulary lightly while extending solidarity to those who suffer.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Making It Explicit
attributes.descriptionShort: Brandom's systematic inferentialist account of conceptual content, linguistic practice, and semantic normativity. Draws explicitly on Kant, Hegel, Frege, and Sellars.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Mind and World
attributes.descriptionShort: Based on the 1991 John Locke Lectures. Argues that the conceptual is unbounded: experience already has conceptual form, dissolving the oscillation between the Myth of the Given and coherentism.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Intention
attributes.descriptionShort: The founding text of the philosophy of action. Analyzes intentional action as action under a description; introduces the distinction between reasons and causes, anticipating Davidson's work.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Modern Moral Philosophy
attributes.descriptionShort: Coins the term "consequentialism," argues that "moral obligation" without God is an empty concept, and calls for the abandonment of Kantian and utilitarian ethics in favor of an Aristotelian account of human flourishing.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Natural Goodness
attributes.descriptionShort: Grounds ethics in a naturalistic account of what counts as good for living things of a given kind. What is good for a human is what is good for a human as the kind of thing it is — a rational, social, political animal.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Ethics and the Limits of Philosophy
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues that moral philosophy — particularly its systematic varieties (utilitarianism, Kantian ethics) — cannot deliver what it promises: a theory of how to live. Ethics is thicker and more personal than theory allows.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: A Theory of Justice
attributes.descriptionShort: Revives social contract theory via the "veil of ignorance" thought experiment to derive two principles of justice: equal basic liberties, and fair equality of opportunity with the difference principle.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Political Liberalism
attributes.descriptionShort: Reframes justice as fairness as a political rather than comprehensive doctrine, addressing how citizens holding conflicting but reasonable comprehensive views can nonetheless agree on principles of justice.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Anarchy, State, and Utopia
attributes.descriptionShort: The most sophisticated defense of libertarianism in the analytic tradition. Argues that only a minimal state can be justified, and that redistribution violates individual rights.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: After Virtue
attributes.descriptionShort: Diagnoses the fragmentation of modern moral discourse as the consequence of abandoning the Aristotelian teleological framework, and proposes a return to virtue ethics embedded in living traditions.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Fragility of Goodness
attributes.descriptionShort: A philosophical reading of Greek tragedy and Plato on moral luck, vulnerability, and the relationship between ethics and literature. Argues that the good life is constitutively vulnerable.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Animal Liberation
attributes.descriptionShort: Founded the modern animal rights movement by arguing that the capacity to suffer, not species membership, is the morally relevant criterion. Speciesism is analogous to racism and sexism.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Practical Ethics
attributes.descriptionShort: Applies utilitarian reasoning to a range of practical issues: poverty and global aid, abortion, infanticide, euthanasia, and the environment. Highly influential and highly controversial.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Sources of the Self
attributes.descriptionShort: A historical and philosophical account of the modern identity. Argues that modern selfhood is constituted by moral frameworks (sources of the self) that are usually inarticulate and that secular humanism is not self-sufficient.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Speech Acts
attributes.descriptionShort: A systematic development of Austin's speech act theory. Analyzes the distinction between regulative and constitutive rules, and the conditions for successful illocutionary acts.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Intentionality
attributes.descriptionShort: Applies speech act theory's insights to the philosophy of mind, arguing that intentional mental states have conditions of satisfaction analogous to speech acts, and that intentionality is a biological feature of the brain.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Logic of Scientific Discovery
attributes.descriptionShort: Proposes falsifiability as the criterion of demarcation between science and non-science. Theories are scientific not because they can be verified but because they can in principle be refuted.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Open Society and Its Enemies
attributes.descriptionShort: A sustained attack on the "historicist" philosophies of Plato, Hegel, and Marx as providing the intellectual foundations for totalitarianism. Defends liberal democracy and piecemeal social engineering.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Structure of Scientific Revolutions
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues that science progresses not by gradual accumulation but through paradigm shifts — revolutionary episodes where one incommensurable framework replaces another. Introduced "paradigm" and "normal science" into common use.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Reasons and Persons
attributes.descriptionShort: Part III argues that personal identity over time is not what matters; Part IV poses the "non-identity problem" and the "repugnant conclusion" as challenges to any moral theory about future people.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The View from Nowhere
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues that the objective-subjective tension — between the view from nowhere and the view from here — is irreducible and generates genuine philosophical problems about free will, knowledge, and ethics.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: What Is It Like to Be a Bat?
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues that subjective experience — "what it is like" to be something — cannot be captured by objective physical description, posing a fundamental challenge to physicalist accounts of mind.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Consciousness Explained
attributes.descriptionShort: Defends a heterophenomenological, functionalist account of consciousness — the "multiple drafts" model — against both Cartesian theater views and mysterian positions. Highly controversial in both directions.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: The Conscious Mind
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues via the conceivability of zombies that consciousness cannot be explained in purely physical terms. Defends a form of naturalistic dualism: phenomenal properties are ontologically fundamental.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Knowledge and Its Limits
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues that knowledge is a mental state (not merely a state of justified true belief), that knowledge is the norm of assertion, and that the margins for error in belief-formation explain the phenomena that motivate contextualism.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: From Metaphysics to Ethics
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues for the method of conceptual analysis and its indispensability to philosophy; shows how physicalism can vindicate common-sense psychology through the notion of realization.

attributes.label: Work
attributes.title: Essence and Modality
attributes.descriptionShort: Argues that essence — understood as real definition — is not reducible to modal properties: it is not essential to Socrates that he belongs to the singleton {Socrates}, even though this is necessary. Rehabilitated essentialism as a respectable metaphysical doctrine.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: A History of Greek Philosophy, Vol. 1–2
attributes.author: W.K.C. Guthrie
attributes.description: The definitive scholarly history of Greek philosophy from Thales to the Socratics. Provides indispensable historical context for reading both the Presocratics and Plato.
attributes.note: You don't need to read all six volumes. Vol. 1 (Presocratics) and Vol. 4 (Plato, earlier period) are the most useful entry points.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: Fragments
attributes.author: Heraclitus
attributes.description: ~125 surviving fragments articulating universal flux, the unity of opposites, and the Logos as the rational principle governing all change. The foundational challenge to any philosophy of permanence.
attributes.note: Read with a facing-Greek edition if possible. The Kahn translation and commentary (1979) is excellent. These fragments are short but dense — read slowly.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: On Nature
attributes.author: Parmenides
