attributes.description: Survives in fragments. The Way of Truth argues that genuine Being is one, unchanging, and indivisible — directly opposing Heraclitus. The Way of Seeming offers a cosmology as 'merely plausible.' The ur-text of Western metaphysics.
attributes.note: The poem is difficult. Read Heraclitus first so Parmenides' direct opposition registers. The McKirahan translation in 'Philosophy Before Socrates' is reliable.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: Apology
attributes.author: Plato
attributes.description: Plato's account of Socrates' trial and defence. Introduces the Socratic mission, the examined life, and the equation of virtue with knowledge. Only 35 pages — the best entry point to Plato.
attributes.note: Read this before anything else by Plato. The G.M.A. Grube translation (Hackett) is standard.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: Euthyphro
attributes.author: Plato
attributes.description: A brisk Socratic dialogue on the definition of piety. Famous for the Euthyphro dilemma: is the pious loved by the gods because it is pious, or is it pious because it is loved? A masterclass in Socratic refutation.
attributes.note: Companion to the Apology — set on the day Socrates arrives at court. About 20 pages. Introduces Socratic 'What is X?' questions.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: Meno
attributes.author: Plato
attributes.description: Can virtue be taught? Meno is the crucial hinge between the early and middle periods: it introduces the Theory of Recollection (all learning is remembering) and points toward the Forms. The slave-boy geometry proof is one of philosophy's most famous passages.
attributes.note: This is the bridge dialogue. Reading it carefully will make Phaedo and the Republic click.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: Phaedo
attributes.author: Plato
attributes.description: Set on the day of Socrates' death. Argues for the immortality of the soul through the Theory of Recollection and the Theory of Forms. Presents the 'affinity argument' and the final proof from the Form of Life. A pivotal middle-period dialogue.
attributes.note: Contains the first full statement of the Theory of Forms. Read Meno first for the Theory of Recollection which Phaedo presupposes.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: Symposium
attributes.author: Plato
attributes.description: Seven speeches on Eros (love), culminating in Socrates' account of the 'Ladder of Beauty' — the ascent from love of a particular body to love of the Form of Beauty itself. Plato's most literary work and his clearest account of philosophical aspiration.
attributes.note: Can be read alongside Phaedo. The Diotima speech in the Symposium is the aesthetic counterpart to Phaedo's metaphysical argument for Forms.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: An Introduction to Plato's Republic
attributes.author: Julia Annas
attributes.description: The standard philosophical guide to the Republic. Covers the tripartite soul, justice, the Form of the Good, and the critique of poetry. More philosophically rigorous than most introductions.
attributes.note: Read this before or alongside the Republic itself. Annas is clear and critical — she helps you see where Plato's arguments succeed and where they strain.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: Republic
attributes.author: Plato
attributes.description: Plato's masterwork on justice, the ideal city, the philosopher-king, and the Form of the Good. Contains the Allegory of the Cave, the divided line, the tripartite soul, and a sustained critique of democracy. The central text of the Western philosophical tradition.
attributes.note: Don't rush. Budget 4–6 weeks. The Grube/Reeve (Hackett) or Shorey (Loeb) translations are most used. Books II–IV on justice and VII on the Cave are the core; don't skip Books VIII–IX on the decline of the soul.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: Aristotle: A Very Short Introduction
attributes.author: Jonathan Barnes
attributes.description: A brisk, philosophically sharp overview of Aristotle's life, method, and major doctrines by the foremost Aristotle scholar in the Anglophone tradition.
attributes.note: Under 100 pages. Read before the Organon to orient yourself in Aristotle's project.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: Organon (Categories & Posterior Analytics)
attributes.author: Aristotle
attributes.description: The Categories introduces Aristotle's ten-fold classification of what there is (substance, quantity, quality, etc.) — essential for understanding his metaphysics. The Posterior Analytics explains his theory of scientific knowledge and demonstration.
attributes.note: You don't need all six works of the Organon for this curriculum. Focus on Categories (short, critical) and dip into Posterior Analytics I. The Prior Analytics (formal syllogistic) can be skipped on a first pass.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: Nicomachean Ethics
attributes.author: Aristotle
attributes.description: Aristotle's systematic account of the good life, virtue, practical wisdom (phronesis), friendship, and eudaimonia (human flourishing). The most influential work in ancient ethics and the foundation of virtue ethics.
attributes.note: Books I, II, VI, and X are the essential spine. The Irwin (Hackett) translation has excellent notes. Book X's discussion of the contemplative life is in tension with Books I–IX — sit with that tension.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: Metaphysics
attributes.author: Aristotle
attributes.description: Aristotle's investigation of 'Being qua Being' — substance, form, matter, actuality, potentiality, and the Unmoved Mover. Books VII–IX (on substance) and XII (on the divine intellect) are the philosophical summit.
attributes.note: This is the hardest text in the curriculum. Read Categories and NE Ethics first. Books I and II are historiographical — skip to VII for the core. Ross's older translation is available free; Bostock (Clarendon) is the best philosophical commentary.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: Letter to Menoeceus
attributes.author: Epicurus
attributes.description: A four-page summary of Epicurean ethics: the gods do not concern themselves with us, death is not to be feared, pleasure (understood as the absence of pain and anxiety) is the highest good. The most accessible entry point to Epicureanism.
attributes.note: Short (4 pages) but perfectly compressed. The Bailey translation in 'Epicurus: The Extant Remains' is standard. Pair with the Vatican Sayings and Principal Doctrines.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: Meditations
attributes.author: Marcus Aurelius
attributes.description: A private philosophical journal by a Roman emperor. Applies Stoic principles — living according to nature, the dichotomy of control, the brevity of life — to practical daily life. The most personal expression of Stoicism we have.
attributes.note: The Gregory Hays (Modern Library) translation is highly readable. Can be dipped into non-linearly. Read Books II–IV carefully; they contain the core Stoic practice.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: The Fragility of Goodness
attributes.author: Martha Nussbaum
attributes.description: A philosophical reading of Greek tragedy and Plato on moral luck, vulnerability, and the relationship between ethics and literature. Argues that the good life is constitutively vulnerable — Aristotle against the Platonic flight from contingency.
attributes.note: Demanding but rewarding. Best after both Plato's Republic and Aristotle's NE Ethics. The chapters on the Symposium and Plato's 'ascent' are particularly illuminating.

attributes.label: CurriculumItem
attributes.title: After Virtue
attributes.author: Alasdair MacIntyre
attributes.description: Diagnoses the fragmentation of modern moral discourse as a consequence of abandoning the Aristotelian teleological framework. Proposes a return to virtue ethics embedded in living traditions. Made Aristotelian ethics central to contemporary moral philosophy.
attributes.note: Read Chapters 1–9 for the diagnosis of modernity, then 14–18 for the positive Aristotelian proposal. Chapter 15 on the virtues and Chapter 18 on the Aristotelian tradition are the heart of the book.

attributes.description: Plato absorbed Heraclitean flux for the sensible world, assigning stability only to the Forms.
Plato's Parmenides dialogue engages the Eleatic challenge directly; the Forms echo Parmenidean Being.
Epicurus adopted and modified Democritus's atomism, adding the clinamen (swerve) to account for free will.
Aristotle rejected the theory of separated Forms.
Plotinus reads Plato as teaching the hierarchy of the One, Intellect, and Soul.
Augustine read the Neoplatonists before his conversion; their metaphysics of the One shaped his theology of God.
Aquinas's Thomism is an explicit synthesis of Aristotle's metaphysics, physics, and ethics with Christian theology.
Ockham's nominalism is a direct challenge to Thomistic realism about universals.
Buridan inherits Ockham's nominalism and extends his work on supposition theory and the logic of consequences.
Buridan's modal syllogistic is the most consolidated medieval reading of Aristotle's Prior Analytics I.8–22.
Berkeley radicalizes Locke's empiricism by eliminating material substance entirely.
Spinoza adopts Descartes's geometric method and substance metaphysics, then transforms both.
Hume "awoke Kant from his dogmatic slumber."
Rousseau's moral philosophy, especially the general will and respect for persons, shaped Kant's ethics.
Hegel takes the Kantian critical project and argues that the thing-in-itself is an incoherent residue to be dissolved in the Absolute.
Fichte's self-positing I and dialectical method are immediate forerunners of Hegel's dialectic.
Schelling's identity-philosophy is the immediate stimulus the Phenomenology responds to and surpasses.
Schopenhauer accepts the Kantian distinction between phenomena and things-in-themselves, identifying the latter with Will.
Rawls construes his theory as a Kantian account of justice, grounding it in rational autonomy and the categorical imperative.
Kierkegaard's entire project protests the System: it swallows the individual into the universal and leaves no room for the leap of faith.
Marx inverted Hegel's idealist dialectic — the movement of history is driven by material forces, not Spirit.
Nietzsche rejected Hegel's teleological view of history.
Sellars's attack on the Myth of the Given recapitulates Hegel's critique of sense-certainty.
Brandom explicitly draws on Hegel's Phenomenology for his model of recognition and social normativity.
McDowell invokes Hegel's "second nature" to resolve the tension between freedom and nature.
Gadamer's historicism and dialectical model of interpretation inherit Hegelian themes.
Negative Dialectics is in sustained polemic with Hegel's principle of identity.
Nietzsche began as a Schopenhauerian; the will to power is a revision of the Will to Live.
Heidegger read Nietzsche as the culmination and exhaustion of Western metaphysics.
Foucault's genealogical method is explicitly derived from Nietzsche's Genealogy of Morality.
Deleuze's Nietzsche and Philosophy (1962) is one of the most influential interpretations and shapes his own philosophy of difference.
Brentano's concept of intentionality is the starting point for Husserl's phenomenology.
Heidegger was Husserl's assistant; Being and Time transforms phenomenological method into fundamental ontology.
Sartre encountered Husserl's phenomenology in Berlin and it became the method of Being and Nothingness.
Levinas translated Husserl into French and wrote the first French dissertation on his phenomenology.
Gadamer was Heidegger's student; Truth and Method extends Heideggerian hermeneutics.
Derrida's deconstruction is developed largely through a critical reading of Heidegger on language, presence, and metaphysics.
Gadamer engages Dilthey's hermeneutics throughout Truth and Method, both inheriting and criticizing it.
Russell read Frege's Grundgesetze and adopted logicism; Russell's paradox then undermined Frege's system.
Wittgenstein went to Cambridge specifically to work with Russell after reading Frege.
Carnap attended Frege's lectures and his logical syntax program develops Fregean logic.
Dummett's major scholarly project was interpreting and extending Frege's philosophy of language.
Russell supervised Wittgenstein at Cambridge; the Tractatus develops Russell's logical atomism.
The Vienna Circle read the Tractatus as supporting their verificationism.
Anscombe was Wittgenstein's student and literary executor; Intention is a Wittgensteinian work.
Kripke on Rules and Private Language is an interpretation of — and landmark intervention in — the rule-following sections of the Investigations.
"Two Dogmas of Empiricism" is addressed principally at Carnap's analytic-synthetic distinction.
Ayer visited the Vienna Circle and brought logical positivism to a British audience.
Davidson was Quine's student; he inherits naturalism and transforms it with a truth-theoretic semantics.
Quine and Goodman were close colleagues; their joint paper on nominalism is a landmark.
Rorty draws heavily on Davidson's critique of the scheme-content dualism in his neo-pragmatism.
Barcan Marcus's quantified modal logic and direct reference anticipate the core of Naming and Necessity.
Kripke's rigid designators directly influenced Putnam's Twin Earth and natural kind terms.
James acknowledged Peirce as the founder of pragmatism and popularized the movement.
Brandom's inferentialism directly develops Sellars's distinction between the space of reasons and the space of causes.
McDowell's Mind and World is in sustained dialogue with Sellars's "Empiricism and the Philosophy of Mind."
Rorty was Sellars's student; Philosophy and the Mirror of Nature develops Sellarsian themes.
Brandom was Rorty's student at Princeton.
Mill was educated as a Benthamite but revised utilitarianism to distinguish quality of pleasures.
"Modern Moral Philosophy" called for return to Aristotelian virtue ethics.
Natural Goodness grounds ethics in Aristotelian natural teleology.
After Virtue diagnoses modernity's loss of the Aristotelian teleological framework.
Anarchy, State, and Utopia is written as an explicit libertarian response to A Theory of Justice.
Williams critiques Rawlsian moral theory for its detachment from actual human motivations.
Nagel's "What Is It Like to Be a Bat?" directly anticipates Chalmers's hard problem.
Searle was Austin's student; Speech Acts develops Austinian speech act theory systematically.

attributes.note: Historical context before the primary texts
Parmenides directly opposes Heraclitean flux
Plato's Theory of Forms is a response to Parmenides' monism
Flux doctrine motivates Plato's search for stable definitions
Same dramatic context; Euthyphro occurs just before the trial
Establish Socratic method before the bridge dialogue
Both probe definition of a virtue; Meno generalises the puzzle
Recollection doctrine first appears in Meno; Phaedo assumes it
Meno's positive account of virtue as knowledge prepares Symposium's ascent
Theory of Forms fully assumed throughout the Republic
The Form of Beauty in Symposium completes the picture of Forms in Republic
Read secondary prep before the long primary text
Aristotle's Categories directly respond to Platonic Forms
Aristotle's ethics is in dialogue with Plato's conception of the good throughout
Orient in Aristotle's project before the logical works
Categories are the propaedeutic to first philosophy
NE Ethics presupposes the hylomorphic account of human nature
Epicurus' rejection of political life responds to Plato's philosopher-king ideal
Stoic ethical concepts develop in direct awareness of Aristotle's eudaimonism
The Stoics and Epicureans are the twin poles of Hellenistic ethics; read together
Nussbaum's central argument is an Aristotelian critique of Platonic detachment
Nussbaum directly engages the Republic's treatment of tragedy and love
After Virtue is a defence of Aristotelian ethics; NE Ethics is its subject
